raw.regions_all: İspanya (Kuzey Hristiyan krallıkları hariç); Balear Adaları; Kuzey Afrika'da etkili
raw.narrative_en: The Umayyads of al-Andalus, founded by Abd al-Rahman fleeing the Abbasid massacre, built the most brilliant civilization of the Islamic West on the Iberian Peninsula. Cordoba became Europe's largest and most enlightened city in the 10th century, while Abd al-Rahman III adopted the caliphal title, creating an alternative Islamic center to Baghdad. The convivencia tradition of Muslim, Christian, and Jewish coexistence profoundly influenced Western intellectual history.

raw.narrative_en: The Almohads rose through a radical religious reform movement based on Ibn Tumart's doctrine of divine unity, building the largest empire of the Islamic West from the Maghreb to the Iberian Peninsula. While patronizing philosophers like Ibn Rushd and Ibn Tufayl, their defeat at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) initiated the empire's dissolution.

raw.regions_all: Fas; Batı/Orta Cezayir; Endülüs; Sahra; Moritanya
raw.narrative_en: The Almoravids emerged from the veiled Sanhaja warriors of the Sahara, shaped by ribat discipline and spreading Maliki orthodoxy by the sword. Their victory at Zallaqa rescued al-Andalus from Christian advance while building a vast empire spanning the Maghreb to the Iberian Peninsula.

raw.capital: Kayrevân; al-Mahdiyye (Zîrîler); Qal'at Banī Hammād; Bicâye/Bougie (Hammâdîler)
raw.narrative_en: The Zirids and Hammadids were Sanhaja Berber dynasties that transitioned from Fatimid service to independence. Zirid emir al-Mu'izz's declaration of Abbasid allegiance provoked the Fatimids to redirect the Hilalian and Sulaym Bedouin tribes toward the Maghreb, a migration wave that permanently altered North Africa's demographic structure.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Mulūk al-Tawā'if or Reyes de Taifas in Spain
raw.capital: Çeşitli (İşbiliye, Gırnata, Tuleytula, Sarakusta, Belensiye vb.)
raw.narrative_en: The Taifa Kingdoms emerged from the collapse of the Andalusian Umayyad caliphate, producing over thirty independent principalities. While political fragmentation brought military weakness, each court competed to become a center of culture and literature. The fall of Toledo to Alfonso VI in 1085 marked the irreversible beginning of territorial loss for Islamic al-Andalus.

raw.regions_all: Granada; Málaga; Almería; kısmen Cádiz, Jaén, Murcia
raw.narrative_en: The Nasrids ruled Granada for two and a half centuries as the last bastion of Islamic al-Andalus, constructing the Alhambra Palace as one of the finest masterpieces of Islamic architectural art. They survived through masterful balancing between Christian Spain and Marinid Morocco, but internal succession struggles and the Castile-Aragon union rendered resistance futile, leading to their surrender in 1492.

raw.regions_all: Balear Adaları; Doğu Mağrib (İfrîkıye bölgesinde faaliyetler)
raw.narrative_en: The Banu Ghaniya were the last remnant of the Almoravid dynasty in the Balearic Islands, maintaining independence as a resilient maritime principality for eighty years in the Mediterranean. They waged prolonged guerrilla warfare against the Almohads in eastern Maghreb, defending the declining Almoravid cause.

raw.notes: First Islamic dynasty caliphate; Andalusian branch lasted to 1031
